data.title: Spok Holdings: Software Gains Are Still Too Low To Offset Structural Declines
data.description:
Introduction

Spok Holdings’ (SPOK) share value has been cratering this past year, so I thought it would be a great time to revisit it, since I haven’t covered it since October of 2024, when I gave it a hold rating, as there was no rush to start a position. I came to the same conclusion this time around as well. The wireless business will continue to decline, which will affect its cash flow, and the software side of things is taking much too long to offset the structural declines in legacy.

Briefly on performance

Starting from the very top, we can see that the company’s revenue growth stalled in the second half of the year. This is primarily due to the legacy wireless business continually shrinking, while its software booking came in rather choppy as well. The new software gains were just not enough to keep the total revenue accelerating. Back in Q2 of the year, the management was confident enough to raise full-year guidance, but it turns out it was a bit premature, as the second half of the year was softer overall. By Q4, software bookings bounced back sharply, but the damage of H2 was already done.

We can see that due to the overall top-line weakness, the company’s margins were pressured as well. The easy explanation is the costs didn’t fall as fast as its revenue, and the mix was still largely affected by the declining wireless as well as growth in lower-margin services work. The company must have a lot more fixed costs on the books, so if sales suffer, there are fewer units to spread out those costs across. Margins across the board are down around 100bps from the highs set in the second quarter of the year.

And it is not surprising that other key efficiency metrics have followed suit. Since the second quarter, SPOK’s management has ended up utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital less efficiently. The decline isn’t particularly bad, but it is not a good trend. Also, these are below what I would consider decent. Especially ROE and ROTC. It seems like the company is losing pricing power, and its moat is deteriorating.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. SPOK finished the year with a little over $25m in cash and equivalents, against no debt, apart from some lease obligations. This is particularly good for a company that is experiencing slight inefficiencies in operations, like SPOK. A balance sheet like that can help the companies weather the uncertainty and operational issues a lot better than a balance sheet that has to allocate some of the resources to paying down interest on debt or the debt itself. Additionally, we can see that the company’s cash from operations actually bucked the trend we are seeing, as it has, more or less, recovered from Q3 lows.

Its free cash flow was around $25m for the year, which has been very consistent throughout the years. It’s not a bad sign, but it isn’t growing either.

Looking at the past year’s performance, the only thing I can say about SPOK is that this may not have been the year of change and transformation that anyone would have hoped. The management wasn’t able to successfully offset the decline in legacy wireless with its software segment, and that could be a larger issue at hand.

Comments on the Outlook

I think it is safe to say that SPOK needs to fully focus on getting its software adoption up a notch. What it is doing right now hardly makes for decent growth if its legacy wireless continues to decline at a faster pace than its software is growing. Wireless will continue to decline and eventually disappear. It’s structural erosion, something that just has to happen as more and more customers switch to software-based solutions that SPOK is offering. That means I am expecting wireless to be cannibalized by its software segment, as well as customers switching to another provider altogether. The bad thing about this trend, however, is that right now, wireless produces the bulk of operating cash flows relative to its non-growth. That is because this segment is already mature and requires low capex and upkeep. That means most of the revenue is essentially going to trickle down to the bottom line. Software is the growth driver, and it requires a lot of investment, which is going to put a damper on its cash generation for now. I am expecting to see cash from operations suffer in the future, until software investments slow down and the company reaches the inflection point, when the legacy declines won’t be as detrimental as they are right now.

If we dig a little deeper into the revenues of software, we can see that managed services did particularly well this past year, or over the last few years, for that matter. This part of the service that SPOK offers seems to be resonating with the hospitals quite a bit since the growth in revenue here was 134% from ’23 to ’24, and 103% from ’24 to ’25. Granted, it is still only around 10% of the total software revenue, but if SPOK can keep up the momentum, this is going to be a serious contender as the main growth driver. The managed services segment is basically Spok helping customers operate, configure, maintain, and optimize parts of the communication environment for the hospital/customer. No one wants to do this, especially if they are unfamiliar with the platform, and who else is better equipped to optimize if not SPOK themselves?

In order to succeed in this transition, the company needs to show that it can grow its software bookings as well as convert them into actual revenue in the future. The company seems to be doing fine for now, but what happens when it starts to slow down? SPOK won’t see a top-line growth for a while. Booking rose 83% sequentially, which is great. I’d like to see the momentum sustained, so the software business begins to properly offset the losses in wireless and revitalize its top-line performance.

Is SPOK worth an Investment?

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model to see what the company might be worth to us. Below are the assumptions I used.

Revenue growth may be a little optimistic, given that it has been going down recently. Its 5-year CAGR was -0.35%. I’ll stick with 2%, nonetheless.

For margins, I am modeling an improvement overall because as legacy declines, software increases, margin mix improves due to that shift in economics, so it is reasonable to assume that profitability improves. It’s only an improvement of 60bps over the next half-decade, so I am being rather conservative here. The rest I am keeping close to the company’s historical averages for further simplicity. Additionally, as it is a small-cap company that is not very well covered, with low average trading volume, I have decided to use around 8% as my discount instead of the company’s WACC of 7% or so. That way I get a bit of margin of safety. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we get the company’s intrinsic value to be around $10.15 a share, which means SPOK is still trading a bit over its fair value, even after the recent poor stock performance.

Closing Comments

If you have been holding the company since it hit 52-week highs back in August of last year, then I don’t see the point in selling right now. I think the worst has already passed, and all of that is more or less already priced in. The company just needs to show it can successfully revitalize its operations through further software adoption for customers so that the legacy wireless doesn’t dampen the results quite so badly.

I would be looking to initiate a position somewhere in the 9$ range or at least revisit the thesis to see if there has been any fundamental change. But for now, I am going to continue to sit on the fence.

data.title: TurboQuant Fears Overblown, Micron Deserves A Buy
data.description:
Introduction:

What a week for memory. The market was absolutely stunned last week following the news that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) had devised a new algorithmic method, known as TurboQuant, which would reduce the memory requirement for AI workloads by up to a factor of 6. The news sparked a sell off in the big memory companies, including Micron (MU). The narrative behind the sell-off was this new algorithm would fundamentally change the paradigm for memory use in memory-heavy workloads, such as AI, with the end result being less memory demand and a big P&L hit for the likes of Micron.

I find it unlikely that AI developers are going to suddenly decide they need to use less memory. The reason there are memory shortages and bottlenecks at present is due to insatiable underlying demand. A step change in efficiency is a good thing; it means developers are able to achieve more with less, but I think in the arms race to develop the best systems, everybody will still want more. I see the sell-off in memory names as irrational, and I see Micron as a buy rating.

TurboQuant:

The first thing worth acknowledging about the TurboQuant breakthrough, if it's correct to call it that, is that it's a testament to the enduring power of innovation. It never ceases to amaze me the ability of engineers, technicians, and developers to innovate and push technology standards forward. Technology changes over time create winners and losers in the markets, but at the system level, it is ultimately a benefit to economies and markets.

The sell-off in memory stocks post the Google announcement strikes me as a classic knee-jerk market reaction that we see from time to time. The pattern is consistent: some market news comes out that challenges the existing wisdom. Market participants, which these days can often just be trading systems using natural language programs to read through news stories and headlines, see the news come out and react by trading positions immediately. This to me is not investing; it gives no thought or due consideration to the dynamics at play. A recent example of this behavior in the market was the SaaS sell off, which serves as an example of negative headlines for software being followed by indiscriminate selling of anything and everything software. Little thought was given to how much direct exposure firms actually hold.

The current memory situation, to me, looks like a carbon copy of what happened last year with DeepSeek. For those who don't remember, a Chinese AI lab called DeepSeek released a reasoning model called R1 which was developed using a fraction of the compute cost of the leading Western models at the time. Semiconductor stocks took a beating as the prevailing narrative was there would not be the same compute demand, as leading AI developers would be able to do more with less. So no need for incredibly expensive Nvidia (NVDA) Blackwell systems, right?

Exactly, we know how that affair played out. Jevon's Paradox proved to be very much a real thing, and the prevailing narrative shifted to, if we can do more with less, then let's just use more and do even more. Some may disagree with me, but I cannot see why the usage of memory would be any different from what we saw with GPUs last year. Google's algorithm may well have vastly improved the efficiency of memory for AI workloads. What do you think developers are going to do? I feel pretty sure they are going to utilize even more memory, leading to ever greater context windows.

Memory Backdrop:

The global memory market is in a huge supply/demand imbalance right now. The memory market is a classically cyclical market where supply and demand gyrate and create a consistent series of peaks and troughs over time. The AI story has pushed the market into a significant supply deficit at this moment. The key reason for this is the rapid growth of high-bandwidth memory, or HBM which requires up to 4x as many wafers as traditional DRAM. The big AI players, like the hyperscalers, cannot get their hands on enough memory at present, either for their own custom silicon or as an attachment to an Nvidia Blackwell compute rack.

In the past, memory procurement was done on a short term basis, but now, due to the unprecedented level of demand, customers are locking in future demand through longer-term contracts. This change in order patterns clearly signals where the market is at: demand is dominating supply. The longer term ordering also gives memory players unusual levels of visibility into their future revenue streams. Hock Tan, the CEO of Broadcom (AVGO) was noted recently as saying his firm had secured their memory supply as far out as 2028.

I would caution that any talk of an end to the cyclicality of memory should be taken with a pinch of salt. Memory has always followed a cycle, and I expect at some point in the future it will revert back to its natural pattern. What we are seeing right now is just a very unusual cycle with an incredible amount of demand supporting a sustained upside momentum. Given we see such a strong demand signal and multiyear contracts, I think this supports a buy thesis on Micron over the medium term.

Valuation

Micron's valuation vs. history is either incredibly attractive or incredibly unattractive, depending on what metric you apply. Why do I say that? Well, if we look at it on a forward P/E basis, as seen below, it looks like an easy pick. The current P/E is only 4x, and it's well below recent averages.

There is a catch, of course; the chart above is only a one-year P/E chart, and I chose that for good reason. Take a look at the long-term P/E chart below, which goes back to the mid-90s. As you can see, it's totally whacky; the P/E jumps all over the place. The reason for this is the extreme cyclical nature of the memory market, as I discussed above. Earnings in the industry swing between highs and lows; this makes it tough to use a price-to-earnings metric as a way to gauge the valuation of a company.

The classic way to value memory stocks is to employ a price-to-book approach. This approach strips out the volatility inherent in earnings and looks at the equity or book value of the company's balance sheet. Price-to-book is not without limitation in my mind; rather than looking ahead at earnings to come, it focuses on the accounting value of a firm, which is a backward-looking metric. It's not perfect, but it is much steadier. Based on this methodology, Micron is pretty obviously expensive.

Micron is expensive, but it has become pretty clear over the past year or so that the demand for memory is in a different paradigm compared to any other period in history. The unusual use of long-term contracts by customers signals a desperation to lock in supply, and Micron is essentially sold out for years to come. Hence I think the firm should trade at an expensive valuation; it's appropriate for the moment. I don't think the wave of demand will last forever in memory, but it does look set to continue for at least 3-5 years, and I think Micron stands to do very well during that time-frame.

Risks

Micron naturally plays a role in the AI ecosystem. As capex dollars flow out from the hyperscalers, they naturally land as revenue at companies such as Nvidia or the memory players like Micron. The revenue coming from the big hyperscaler capital spending has come to dominate the revenue and earnings outlook for Micron in a way that is not normal. Of course it isn't normal; we are living through the age of the initial AI buildout, a phase of incredible infrastructure spending, so it makes sense why this revenue stream has come to dominate other traditional streams such as memory for smartphones or PCs.

The question really is, at what point do the hyperscalers say we have enough infrastructure built, the peak has passed, and now we plan to rein in our capex in order to focus more on things like shareholder returns? Once that point is hit, I believe the revenue and earnings opportunity for a company like Micron could fundamentally change. I mentioned memory is a cyclical industry with peaks and troughs. The current trajectory is towards a very high peak, maybe the highest in the history of the industry, but what follows will naturally be a steep decline.

When will that point come? I don't know. What I do know is the memory market is reported to be completely sold out for years, and customers have been seeking to enter into longer-term contracts, something not typical of the industry. So that tells me the peak is likely still a while away. Given the uncertainty, though, I would advise readers that appropriate position sizing is key, Micron represents a great opportunity, and I think there is still room to run, but choose a position size that adequately balances the opportunity with the risk.

Conclusion

I think the market reaction to the Google TurboQuant news was a rinse and repeat of what we saw last year with DeepSeek. The market clearly hasn't learned the lessons of that episode. We saw clearly last year that the desire for compute for AI is insatiable, and that holds true for memory in my mind too. The memory demand is clearly evidenced by an industry that has capacity sold out, I do not think a change in memory efficiency will change the underlying level of demand one bit.

I feel pretty sure the leading AI labs want as much memory as they can possibly get a hold of, and they all know that's what their rivals think too, increasing the urgency. With this very strong demand backdrop, I think Micron has room to run over the medium term, and I see the pullback as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Can Any Investor Actually Value SpaceX?
data.description:
Why this IPO is historic:

SpaceX (SPACE) is back in the headlines again, and this time the catalyst is one to really look at. The company confidentially filed its draft IPO registration with the SEC on Wednesday, April 1st, putting it on track for what could be a June listing. If it prices near expectations, it will be the largest public offering in history.

SpaceX could seek a valuation of over $1.75 trillion, a number that would top every prior IPO and make it the first company to debut on public markets above the trillion-dollar mark. Even talks of a possible IPO sent aerospace players in the sector flying last year, as seen below.

Once again, the announcement is sending shockwaves across the aerospace sector, even as the broader market sells off on geopolitical tension.

The SpaceX IPO filing lifted the entire space basket on Thursday, with Planet Labs (PL) up 9.49%, Satellogic (SATL) up 8.06%, Viasat (VSAT) up 7.90%, Intuitive Machines (LUNR) up 6.87%, Echostar (SATS) up 2.55%, Firefly Aerospace (FLY) up 1.81%, Rocket Lab (RKLB) up 1.65%, and AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) up 1.36%.

To put the fundraising in perspective: the company is reportedly targeting up to $75 billion in proceeds, more than three times the size of Saudi Aramco's $29 billion debut in 2019, which has stood as the record for seven years.

This time around, though, SpaceX is entering the market considerably larger and more complex than the rocket business many investors originally tracked in private markets. SpaceX merged with Elon Musk's xAI (X.AI) earlier this year, creating a combined entity spanning aerospace, satellite internet, social media via X, and AI, with Musk having announced ambitions to deploy a network of one million orbital satellites serving as AI data centers. Revenue is approaching $20 billion in 2026, with the launch program and Starlink driving the bulk of it, while xAI is expected to contribute less than $1 billion in the near term.

What you’re buying:

SpaceX is considering a dual-class share structure that would give insiders, including Musk, extra voting power, and up to 30% of the offering could be allocated to retail investors. Note that the latter is an unusually large retail tranche, signaling that the company is deliberately cultivating a broad shareholder base from day one.

The launch business remains the brand anchor, mainly through reusable Falcon 9 rockets. The latter’s reusability program has fundamentally restructured the economics of getting to orbit. By recovering and reusing first-stage boosters, with one having now completed 34 missions as of early 2026, SpaceX has driven its internal launch costs down to somewhere in the $15 million to $30 million range per mission, against a comparable figure closer to $80 million for traditional competitors.

But launch is increasingly the infrastructure layer that funds everything else, not the growth story itself. The growth story is Starlink, and I’ll get to that in a second.

Now, let’s look at the numbers. The company’s revenue has been on a steep upward trajectory, from $13.1 billion in 2024 to roughly $15 billion in 2025, with analysts projecting a jump to nearly $24 billion in 2026, around 50% year-over-year growth. Starlink contributed about $10.4 billion in 2025, nearly 70% of total company revenue, and according to Payload, that number is expected to nearly double to $18.7 billion this year, which would push Starlink's share of consolidated revenue to roughly 80%.

The real, recurring revenue engine:

While the Falcon 9 program commands the most attention, the actual earnings story at SpaceX is Starlink. The latter crossed 10 million subscribers in February, adding its most recent million users in under 53 days. Starlink is now approaching 10,000 satellites in low-Earth orbit, serving millions of customers globally, with revenue scaling rapidly toward the majority of the company's top line.

That subscriber base now spans more than 155 countries, with the majority of customers located outside the U.S. Geographic diversification meaningfully reduces concentration risk for prospective public-market investors. Starlink's user base has been doubling roughly every year, from 2.3 million at end of 2023, to 4.6 million at end of 2024, to 9.2 million by end of 2025, and the consensus is now around 18.4 million by end of 2026.

What makes this particularly interesting from a cash flow perspective is the margin inflection underway. Gross margins that were in the low single digits just two years ago are expected to approach 25% in 2026, as the capital-intensive satellite buildout phase gives way to high-margin recurring subscription revenue.

The competitive moat deserves serious credit, too. Starlink's first-mover advantage in low-Earth orbit, along with its vertical integration advantage that no rival can replicate, as it launches its own satellites on its own rockets. Add the FCC's approval of an additional 7,500 second-generation satellites and a direct-to-cell capability that integrates with standard smartphones, and the addressable market expands far beyond, and into global mobile connectivity.

How to value SpaceX:

The first problem in valuing the stock is that there's no obvious ticker to benchmark it against. The company has eggs in 4 baskets, and they trade at wildly different multiples and attract entirely different investor bases.

If you frame SpaceX as an aerospace and defense name, the natural competitors are Boeing (BA) and Lockheed Martin (LMT), both of which trade at 1-2x revenue. At that multiple, SpaceX's projected 2026 revenue of $24 billion implies a valuation of around $30-50 billion, which would make the $1.75 trillion IPO target look absurdly huge, and it’s safe to say no one is using that framework. If you frame it as a telecom play anchored on Starlink's recurring subscription revenue, you'd look at satellite operators like Viasat (VSAT), which is up around 14% on Thursday alone, or traditional broadband providers, which trade at 2-4x revenue, still nowhere near justifying a $1.75 trillion valuation on current financials.

To defend this valuation, one must frame Starlink as a hypergrowth technology platform, closer to how the market valued early Netflix (NFLX) or Spotify (SPOT), where you're paying for future subscriber penetration and margin expansion. On that framework, a 70-80x forward revenue multiple becomes the conversation, which is roughly where the IPO valuation math lands.

Then there's the xAI layer, which introduces an entirely separate AI infrastructure narrative that’s loosely related to hyperscalers and GPU cloud plays. What I think will end up happening is that SpaceX will be valued as a Musk premium conglomerate, a sum-of-parts story.

Risks I’m watching:

For all the consumer-facing appeal of Starlink and the excitement around xAI, a meaningful portion of SpaceX's business is underwritten by the federal government, and that's a risk profile worth understanding before the IPO.

The company has collected over $24 billion in government contracts since 2008, spanning NASA cargo and crew missions, Space Force national security launches, and a Pentagon contract signed in April 2025 worth $5.9 billion for 28 national security launch missions through 2029. That revenue is predictable and high-margin, which is why it's been a core part of the bull case in the private markets.

The risk is that it's also inherently political. SpaceX's largest single customer is the U.S. government, and while Musk has stepped back from his formal role in DOGE, his political profile remains elevated, and his relationship with the current administration continues to carry weight, for better or worse.

Why I’m bullish nonetheless:

My bullish take on SpaceX rests on two things that are difficult to find in a single public equity: optionality and the absence of a credible single competitor.

What you're really buying here is several large markets in one stock. Starlink alone takes a lot of the weight, but layer on xAI's orbital data center ambitions, direct-to-cell mobile connectivity, and the long-term Starship economics, and you have a company that could look radically different, and radically larger, in five years than it does today. A company that would need to be beaten on four different fronts at once is, in practice, very difficult to disrupt.

data.title: Allstate Has Weathered The Insurance Storm In 2025, As Its Umbrella Remains Strong
data.description:
An analogy I love to use about the insurance sector, since springtime is when many people go sailing or boating, is that an experienced skipper should anticipate the risk of potential storms or sudden wind changes before even leaving the harbor, which is calm water and often elusive.

Allstate Corp. (ALL) is like that skipper and is ultimately in the business of managing risk before it occurs. A property & casualty/P&C insurer around since the 1930s, according to its SA profile, it considerably beat its last 4 earnings estimates, which is impressive.

Its stock is also up around +76% since I first recommended holding on to it in my Sept. 2023 article, and also up since my buy rating in May 2025. In both cases, I was motivated by the dividend growth, strong credit ratings, and market share within the P&C niche.

In this follow-up, nearly a year since my last, I preview this stock ahead of its late-April earnings call and consider additional angles I didn't before, combining both fundamental and technical analysis points.

Thesis Summary

After my updated research, for my 1-year anniversary coverage of this stock, I'm reaffirming my prior buy rating again, agreeing with the latest buy consensus (as of Thursday) from Wall St. and SA analysts.

Although many fundamental and technical points about Allstate were driving more of a neutral view, what really helped move the needle just into bullish territory for me was growth of new insurance policies, an acceptable balance sheet risk profile, and a convincing dividend story.

The above worksheet shows how my 8 rating categories contributed to the overall bullish score. Read on to see my actionable insights in each category.

Macro & Sector Trends

To start off, when considering the larger macro and sector environment, I gave this section a hold rating after considering cost inflation, stiff competition, and pricing pressure.

While the P&C insurance niche may seem like it has a natural market demand due to the need to insure cars and homes, and therefore a guaranteed upside, let's step back and consider there is price pressure and also many insurers trying to get the business of auto & home customers.

In fact, S&P Global wrote in January:

A softening pricing environment and increased competition in the personal auto space are among the issues facing US property and casualty insurers in 2026.

Additionally, as we headed into 2026, a major agency like Fitch was "neutral" on the P&C sector rather than bullish, saying in a December note that while they expect stability, they also see headwinds for the sector:

macro risks—including increasing competition, geopolitical uncertainty, slowing economic growth and a difficult legal environment—could pose challenges for pricing discipline, reserve adequacy, and claims management.

This sector has been under some increased cost pressure. Although insuring cars is not the only niche for Allstate, consider from Allstate's last investor deck (pg 5), that auto insurers can get hit with some of the following costs, including physical damage and injury:

Many of those costs have risen in recent years, with that same investor deck saying, "Physical damage costs have increased ~47% over 5 years," and "Injury costs have increased ~52% over 5 years."

Competitive Positioning

Moving on, in thinking about how Allstate and its stock fit among peers, I am confident but neutral, so I gave it a hold rating here, after considering both historic performance vs. peers and also market rank.

For comparable peers, I picked US publicly traded P&C insurers like Progressive (PGR), Travelers (TRV), and The Hartford (HIG). Along with Allstate, this peer group is in the market cap range of $30B - $120B.

Other key peers were left out of this comparison but are worth noting: Geico is a major peer in the US but is a subsidiary of conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B), while peers like State Farm and Liberty Mutual are not publicly traded.

In considering historical performance, according to peer data, Allstate impressed with a 5-year revenue CAGR of +10%, putting it 2nd in this peer group behind Progressive, while its ROE of +39.4% almost puts it neck-in-neck with Progressive. I think both of these show Allstate can be a serious competitor on both the top and bottom line, within this peer group, and investors will notice.

As far as historic share price performance, using momentum data, I compared Allstate and its peers vs. the S&P 500 as a major benchmark, and although it didn't beat the benchmark, it did see a 10-year price return of just over +200%, while its major peer Progressive easily beat the benchmark.

So, it seems the common thread here is that Progressive is a major force to reckon with in this niche, and Allstate investors should consider that.

Beyond these figures above, to add another angle to this story, the NAIC (Natl. Assoc. Of Insurance Commissioners) ranked Allstate #4 in its 2025 list of P&C insurers, putting it behind Progressive but also non-public firms like State Farm and Berkshire:

While the above rankings do not hurt Allstate, as it is in the top 5, it also sheds light on the fact that there are a few key brands with significant dominance in this niche and considerably higher premiums written.

Company Growth Factors

In this part, I considered a narrower focus than the macro/market environment, and from company-specific data on growth, I gave this stock a strong buy in this section, considering revenue growth trends and new policies sold.

Insurance at its core is actually an easier business to understand than it may seem like, since new insurance policies sold today can drive future upside through revenue from insurance premiums, sort of like a subscription.

With that said, I was interested in the organic growth of policies at Allstate, and notable from the Q4/FY25 results (pg 1) back in early February, "total policies in force increased to 210.9MM in the fourth quarter, up 3.0% from the prior year."

I focus on organic growth in policies more than just revenue growth, since revenue growth can also come from charging higher insurance rates, not just winning new customers, so it may not paint the entire growth picture.

On that front, from the Q4 investor deck (pg. 7), we can see that Allstate has been on a growth trend from 2019 to 2025, seeing considerable growth in personal insurance lines:

It clearly shows in top-line results, too, with income statement data from Seeking Alpha showing that Allstate has an impressive trend of 8 straight years of growth in premiums/annuity revenue since Dec. 2017:

Thus, Allstate has not simply been on a single-quarter growth spurt but has proven it can achieve a steady growth trend, and while this may be based on historic data, I would point out also that forward-looking metrics are also positive, with the FWD revenue growth expected to be +5.4%

Earnings Outlook

Next, let's talk about the earnings picture for Allstate, and after considering factors like operating margins but also the analyst consensus and what could impact earnings, I gave it a hold rating in this section.

A great indicator of core business earnings efficiency is the operating margin, and in this peer group, Allstate saw a margin of +17.7%, actually making it the leader, and you'll also notice from the trend line (dark blue) that it saw a considerable improvement trend over the last year:

I would consider this an upside factor, especially if that trend continues, and that is something I would look for in the upcoming late-April earnings call.

The consensus should also be considered since it shows a mixed picture on this stock, as on the one hand, it gave Allstate 20 upward earnings revisions (vs. 0 downward), yet it is still expecting negative EPS growth this year and next, which I think could dampen the bullishness of many investors and analysts.

While Allstate can be impacted by interest expenses and losses on its investment portfolio, I want to focus on a major cost driver, and that is paying out policy benefits, and these can spike in certain quarters, particularly after catastrophic events damaging homes and autos. For instance, income statement data shows a major spike in benefits paid in the quarter-ending March 2025:

Incidentally, that was the quarter with the major California wildfires, and CNN had reported that Allstate took a huge hit because of it.

I bring this up to help better understand how this niche can see earnings swings if certain quarters see major weather or fire events, as opposed to life insurance, which is impacted by mortality events rather than property damage.

We can see how that impacts a key insurance metric, catastrophe losses, which spiked in the homeowner insurance niche for Allstate by 10% in 2025 vs. 2024, according to the last investor deck (pg 3).

While an insurer cannot with certainty predict catastrophic events far into the future and has to rely on statistical/historic data on regions where it does business, it certainly can be impacted by them, and so I think investors should continue to expect some unpredictability there and potentially fluctuating earnings performance.

Balance Sheet Risk

Moving away from the income statement now and considering the balance sheet, I gave Allstate a modest buy rating after considering its D/E vs. peers, credit ratings, and asset risk exposure.

While some sectors I've covered, like REITs, often have very high debt-to-equity due to heavy use of leverage, this particular niche appears much more conservative about that, and we can see that in the D/E metrics, with Allstate having a D/E of 0.25 which is relatively in line with this peer group, but also has been declining, and that is a great sign:

Second, I am interested in what major credit rating agencies have to say, and the good news is that Fitch reaffirmed an investment-grade BBB+ rating in January. In its December note, Fitch said it "expects positive underwriting results to persist into 2026."

Last May, when S&P Global also reaffirmed its investment-grade rating, it said that they "view Allstate's liquidity as adequate, and do not expect any liquidity constraints to keep it from meeting its obligations in the next 12 months."

I believe risk-focused investors will appreciate not only the low D/E but also the favorable agency ratings above, and this could help support the share price.

On the asset side, a considerable asset risk exposure can be seen in Q4 results (pg. 7), where fixed-income securities make up the large majority of Allstate's investment portfolio, to the tune of $59B:

This could lead to interest rate risk, but also market risk since those bonds can fluctuate in value. We see from Allstate's income statement, for instance, that they had to sell investments at considerable losses in the quarters from Dec. 2024 to June 2025.

It is not something unique to Allstate, though, as most of the insurers I've covered here have this type of scenario, so I wouldn't pick on Allstate simply for holding so much fixed income, especially since it drives a lot of interest income for the firm. For instance, in the quarter ending Dec. 2025, they saw $752MM in interest/dividend income.

Hence, the stronger weight here should be given to the credit ratings and low D/E, and for this reason I am bullish in this section. What may have raised the risk profile and lowered the bullish view would have been significant exposure to alternative investments, certain commercial real estate, and private credit markets.

Dividend Outlook

We've come to talk about dividends, and as a dividend investor myself, I gave this stock a buy rating in this section after considering the several factors below.

Notable in this category is that Seeking Alpha reported back in February that Allstate is raising its dividend by 8% and also doing a major share buyback program. Both of these could certainly drive shares up, but since it is already April, those factors may have already been baked into the price by now.

So, from a more long-term view, I considered yield, growth, and dividend sustainability factors too.

The dividend yield hovering around 2% or so is somewhere behind the sector average, and a far cry from some of the high-yield (above 10%) mortgage REITs I've covered in the last year, so it would not help my portfolio if I was only looking to grow my average yield.

However, with a 10-year dividend CAGR of +12.7%, Allstate has shown it can continue steady dividend growth despite its spikes in claim expenses in specific quarters, and it also got through the pandemic years without a dividend cut, it seems, unlike some sectors that saw a significant impact.

What is really notable is the payout ratio of just 11.4%, showing that Allstate has a significant "buffer" and this is especially important in a major insurer.

So, while not a high-yield stock, its growth case and low payout ratio, as well as its recent dividend hike and share buyback strategy, support a bullish view.

Valuation Metrics

Next, let's discuss the following worksheet I made with inputs from Seeking Alpha data, and from this evaluation, I gave Allstate a hold rating in this section.

While the stock appears quite undervalued vs. peers, I also expect a limited +2% upside, hurting the bullish case here and moving the needle into the hold column.

Although I've shown how Allstate has an impressive operating margin, it also has a negative earnings consensus, and a considerable fixed-income portfolio whose bond values can remain lower in high-interest rate environments. There could also be concern about further weather/fire catastrophe events this year, potentially hitting Allstate hard again.

Technical Charting

In this final section, I moved away from fundamental topics and considered technical patterns found in the price chart below, and based on that, I gave this section a hold rating.

What sticks out right away is that this share price has shown considerable volatility, and not a steady bullish or bearish trend over the last year. It also teeters between the two moving averages continually.

Also, there seems to be a "coiled spring" pattern as the two moving averages are very tight and the share price teeters between them, with no clear bullish or bearish momentum. So, I am neutral here.

Nevertheless, although it brings down the stock's overall score, it was not enough to keep it from being overall a buy.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

A risk to my bullish view, that has not already been mentioned in this article or included in my score, could come from regulatory risk.

Insurance is highly regulated, and in US states it cannot just arbitrarily hike up rates without filing this with the state authority that regulates insurance. This can put a squeeze on what an insurer would like to do vs. what they actually are approved to do.

However, it is not a risk limited to Allstate but its peers also, so major peers like Progressive will likely be in the same scenario. Earlier, I said that growth in premiums can come from organically selling new policies but also from charging higher rates, so this topic certainly can impact the top-line.

Conclusion

To conclude my follow-up on Allstate, I have shown why I would still rate this a buy even a year later and why I see it as not only a dividend income idea but also a long-term growth idea too.

In upcoming earnings in late April, I expect more of the same positive trends and, in particular, will be focusing on the continuing upward trend in the operating margin.

data.title: Hut 8: Why The River Bend Expansion Justifies A Buy Rating
data.description:
In my recent articles, I have explored the ongoing pivot of crypto miners diversifying toward HPC data centers, covering companies like Applied Digital (APLD), IREN Limited (IREN), MARA Holdings (MARA), CleanSpark (CLSK) and TeraWulf (WULF). I was the most bullish on MARA and CleanSpark. I want to continue exploring the sector, with the goal of identifying the most compelling companies. In this article, I will cover Hut 8 (HUT), going over its operating assets, pipeline, and the recent developments. I will concentrate this article on the upcoming HPC business, and not on crypto mining, which is acting as a cash cow, to fund AI data center development. In a way similar to how I evaluated other crypto miners, I will provide valuation estimates, and outline why HUT warrants a ''Buy'' rating.

Introduction and Moat

For those unfamiliar with the company, HUT has been a Bitcoin miner, but has restructured following its late 2023 merger with US Bitcoin Corp. Its majority owned subsidiary, American Bitcoin, now operates the mining business, allowing HUT to focus entirely on AI infrastructure, which is currently experiencing high demand. As long as the infrastructure is in high demand, the company has a data center scarcity moat.

I believe current momentum in the HPC space will continue for the foreseeable future. Data center construction is on the rise, with data center vacancies reaching a record low of 1.4% at year-end, according to CBRE. This, in my opinion, completely negates the bearish theory that we are at the peak of the AI boom. As long as vacancy rates are low, HUT will have a pricing moat when renting out its upcoming data center capacity. It is reasonable to assume that at one point in the future, data center demand will stabilize, at which point we should see commoditization in the sector, eroding this pricing power. But until we see that, I remain bullish on the sector as a whole. Still, the main thing that worries me, is that the company that has the cheapest power available will have an edge over its peers. Based on the assets HUT has, I am a bit skeptical. Still, this is a long-term concern; currently, the overall sector momentum is strong.

Hut 8's Flagship AI Project and Future Expectations

In December 2025, Hut 8 announced a partnership with Anthropic and Fluidstack. The agreement includes a 15-year lease for 245 MW of capacity in Louisiana valued at $7 billion. What makes me bullish on the project is not the value of the deal but the possibility of expansion. The agreed-upon Tranche 2 ''grants the tenant a Right of First Offer (ROFO) for up to 1,000 MW of additional IT capacity at River Bend,'' while Tranche 3 adds a possibility of an additional 1,050 MW of capacity. I believe hyperscalers strongly prefer expanding existing data center campuses, as training large language models requires large compute clusters. Additionally, in my opinion, even for inference, it is beneficial to have a few large data centers rather than a number of small ones, as it is easier to manage a large data center. This makes me believe Anthropic and Fluidstack are poised to lease additional capacity under Tranche 2 and Tranche 3, as long as demand for AI services remains high. And as discussed in the Introduction section, the momentum is currently strong. I believe, that the future of the company lies in this River Bend Campus.

Valuation

To properly evaluate Hut 8, I will project forward to 2029, when, I believe, the rest of River Bend will come online. I will estimate that HUT will successfully lease the entirety of Tranche 2. If the momentum remains exceptionally strong, they might also be able to lease a part of Tranche 3, but I will not include that in my calculation.

In 2029, the initial River Bend tranche will amount to $466 million in annual recurring revenue based on the $7 billion, 15-year lease. That is approx. $1.9 million per MW annually.

If they successfully manage to lease the entirety of Tranche 2 (1000 MW), at the same rate as Tranche 1, that would contribute $1.9 billion in annual revenues. As discussed previously, due to strong HPC data center demand and the tendency of hyperscalers to prefer large data centers at one location, I deem this scenario probable.

Together, the HPC segment would provide the company with $2.366 billion in annual recurring revenues.

Currently, Hut 8 trades at approximately $4.7 billion. If the company achieves my $2.366 billion estimate of HPC revenues, the company would, in 2029 trade at a 2.1x PS multiple.

I believe that once the HPC market matures, and starts to slowly commoditize, HPC companies should be valued in line with the current valuation of legacy cloud and data center REITs. One such example is DigitalOcean Holdings (DOCN), which currently trades at an 8x PS multiple. Another example is Digital Realty Trust (DLR), which trades at a 10x PS multiple. So, even if we apply a conservative 5x sales ending multiple to HUT, we still have a significant upside—138%. If we apply the multiple that Digital Realty is currently trading at, the upside is even higher.

Risks

The main risk, and the reason I am issuing a "Buy" rating instead of a "Strong Buy," is the risk of eventual commoditization, when the price of electricity will be essential. The River Bend project, doesn’t have integrated power generation facilities, but instead relies on the grid. As the grid becomes more congested, the transmission costs should rise, increasing the overall energy cost for the customer. In turn, it could become much harder for Hut 8, to lease the data centers at the $1.9 million per MW rate I used in my calculation. An investor should look closely at data center occupancy rates, to determine when supply will start to catch up with the demand. Again, note that currently, there is no evidence of this happening. But at a certain point in the future, this is poised to happen, and at that moment, companies like MARA will have an advantage. As I discussed in my MARA article, they plan to build both power generation and data centers directly at Permian Basin gas fields, providing them with much cheaper electricity, without grid costs.

The second risk is a macroeconomic slowdown. With rising oil prices, there is a growing fear of rampant inflation, and the possibility of the Fed raising interest rates. This would have a negative impact on high CapEx companies like Hut.

Plan

I am optimistic about Hut 8, and I believe further contracts with hyperscalers for the remaining power capacity, will act as a bullish catalyst. Still, the price is currently historically high, and potential weakness in the macro picture could provide a correction. I believe it would be the best choice to dollar cost average. I plan to open a small starter position and would add to it, if we experience a stronger pullback.

data.title: U.S. Markets Are Repeating 2025's Tantrums
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It’s been a year since the Liberation Day reciprocal tariffs were announced.

The announcement of those tariffs sent markets into a tailspin since there was no clear end in sight to the Trump 2.0 administration’s strategy to “liberate” the U.S. economy from rising trade deficits. The tariff tantrum from last year resulted in markets resolving their differences with the U.S. administration’s rapid back-and-forth trade policy, eventually leading to the markets delivering a 16% gain in CY25.

In this post, I argue that the current geopolitical volatility arising from rapidly evolving narratives from the U.S.'s involvement in the Iran war resembles the incredibly ambiguous environment seen during last year’s tariff tantrums.

I am expecting the markets to resolve their ambiguity with the conflicting narratives from the US/Iran war soon, setting the path for benchmark indices to return in the green this year.

I am optimistic about the S&P 500 (SP500) and deploying my cash accordingly.

Why Markets Are Repeating 2025’s Tariff Tantrum Playbook

Previously, I explained how certain macroindicators, including cryptocurrency price movements, were beginning to trend as if the war were due to end in the next few weeks.

These views were strongly hinted at by the Trump administration a few days ago when it was mentioned that there was a strong desire to “end the conflict in 2-3 weeks.” Those comments by the Trump administration did lead to another relief rally on April 1st, ahead of President Trump’s highly anticipated address to the nation later that day.

Unfortunately, the president's address to the nation left more room to keep the conflict in the Middle East escalated over the next 2-3 weeks, vowing to strike Iran “extremely hard.” In response to President Trump’s comments, oil prices (CL1:COM) jumped 12% on April 2nd.

The spike in oil prices on April 2nd resembled prior surges in oil prices seen on days like last Friday or even earlier this month when concerns about the widening US-Israel-Iran war led to a jump in oil prices, denting the outlook on U.S. equities.

But here is where the divergence in market sentiments appears to be building up.

Trump’s April 1st address to the nation should have shaken the US stock market after he vowed to hit Iran “extremely hard.” Especially after oil prices jumped 12% after the president’s address to the nation. However, I believe the president’s address to the nation represented another set of back-and-forth guidance that appears to be resembling 2025’s Tariff Tantrum operating manual.

As I demonstrate below, markets had begun to recover from the confusing direction last year’s US tariff policy was taking, and that was despite the US/China trade relationship still looking to be on the mend through weeks 15-17 of 2025.

The YTD price action in CY26’s S&P 500 as seen in the red line above, shows that markets were reacting to geopolitical events in a highly similar fashion, comparable to the events unfolding during CY25’s Tariff Tantrum progression last year.

That is until week #14 of this year, i.e., the current week, where markets are stubbornly ignoring >$100 crude oil prices and appear to be pinning their hopes on the Trump administration’s recently announced timelines of ending the war in the next 2-3 weeks.

I’ll demonstrate this in a different way.

As seen in Exhibit B, all asset classes like oil and the US dollar (DXY) that surged in the early days of the US/Israel/Iran war have begun their retreat since the Trump administration announced their 2-3 week deadlines to end the war. Assets like oil futures and the US dollar have retreated, while gold prices (GLD) and the S&P 500 have begun to gain ground.

Even US 10Y bond yields (US10Y), sometimes regarded as an excellent, real-time market indicator of long-term inflation expectations, have given up ground since March 27th, after the deadlines were announced. 10Y bond yields had begun rising in the early days of the war as broader markets began to price in the risks of elevated post-war inflation.

To me, such price action potentially signals that markets have either already bottomed or are in the process of bottoming soon.

This kind of price action is very similar to what we saw in 2025. Markets were incredibly volatile in February and March last year, unable to navigate through the tariff tantrums as the landscape for geopolitical trade kept swinging from positive to negative and vice versa. However, in April last year, the markets sensed initial signs of stability in the U.S. administration’s tariff policy after a 90-day pause in the reciprocal tariffs was announced and eventually bottomed out on April 4th, 2025. Then, throughout April last year, markets proceeded to tune out the noise, heading out of correction territory later in the same month, as illustrated in Exhibit A, despite the US and China not agreeing in principle on resolving their trade disputes at the time. Despite the US/China trade differences, the markets kept moving higher and had begun to price in the risks of the US/China trade dispute in April last year and were expecting these disputes to resolve over those 90 days.

At the moment, I suspect, markets already have or are looking ahead to a global scenario where it's back to business as usual and all parties to this war are able to find consensus on resolving their respective geopolitical disputes, at least for now. I suspect markets are using the 2-3 week deadline that President Trump announced a few days ago to anchor their expectations of thawing geopolitical tensions. Iran itself has begun to strike a more conciliatory tone, coinciding with the U.S. administration’s intention to resolve this dispute within the next 2-3 weeks.

Additionally, markets might also be signaling their intentions to look ahead to a strong roster of exciting IPOs on the horizon. With SpaceX’s $1.75 trillion IPO set for June, and IPOs of OpenAI and Anthropic expected to follow, markets are saying they want the focus to be on the business of capital flow and not on geopolitical instability.

Remember, the S&P 500 is trading at extremely reasonable valuations of just ~20x this year’s projected earnings of $320 per share. That implies projected earnings growth of a massive 18% this year. This kind of high-teens earnings growth has never been seen in the last 5 years, yet markets are trading at a forward valuation of 20x, which is equal to the 5-year PE average of 19.9x and 5% ahead of the 10-year average of 18.9x.

To me, the price action seen in the S&P 500 resembles the setup seen in 2025, and I believe markets have found a way out of the rapidly evolving geopolitical tensions.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

To quote Mark Twain, "History doesn't repeat itself, but it often rhymes.” While my analysis appears to show market action seen so far in CY26 resembling that in CY25, there could be events that could break the pattern.

The elevated risks of geopolitical tensions and conflicts could escalate even further if consensus between parties is not reached. Reports about US fighter jet/jets being shot down and a ceasefire deal falling apart raise the stakes and bring in volatility, leading to more possible disruptions in markets. I will continue to deploy my portfolio capital towards stocks on my watchlist if markets fall further since I continue to expect talks for a ceasefire to eventually land on some form of agreement to end the war. I am still looking at these escalations to end in the next 2-3 weeks, latest by the end of next month.

Additionally, the upcoming midterm elections in the US will likely affect the trajectory of the markets.

Takeaway

Despite the volatility seen so far, I believe markets are finding a way to see through the rapidly changing geopolitical conflict-driven narrative, expecting the tensions to resolve soon. This is similar to the economic trade disputes that arose out of last year’s tariff tantrums, which pressured markets initially but eventually led to markets leading up through the year.

I believe the S&P 500 still looks very appealing given its history and increased projections of earnings. I continue to be bullish on US stocks and am deploying my cash accordingly.

data.title: Starwood Property Trust: The Market Is Handing You An 11% Yield At A Deep Discount
data.description:
I’ve been watching Starwood Property Trust (STWD) trade lower for the better part of a year, and at some point a decision has to be made. I believe that the market has missed the investment narrative on STWD, as it's now sitting around $17.20 per share, which is over a 10% discount to its undepreciated book value of $19.25. It’s below what new investors paid just seven months ago when the company raised equity at $19.70 per share, as the share price has fallen to the point where the dividend yield is exceeding 11%. I think this is an interesting opportunity for long-term investors, as they can allocate capital towards one of the largest commercial mortgage REITs while everyone else is figuring out if the business case is still viable. STWD just reported its Q4 and 2025 year end numbers, which I felt did a great job of setting the stage for the future. STWD deployed $12.7 billion across their platform, which was the second largest investment year in company history, while executing $4.4 billion in capital markets transactions. STWD brought their leverage down while the board authorized a $400 million share buyback, which projects confidence. I think too many people are getting scared by the short-term geopolitical and macroeconomic implications rather than looking at what is likely to happen when the fog of war evaporates. I am adding to my position, and I will gladly continue to lock in a dividend yield that exceeds 11% from STWD when everyone else is running for the hills.

Following up on my previous article about STWD

Back in the middle of December, I had written an article on STWD (can be read here) where I discussed how I continued to buy shares whenever STWD’s dividend yield exceeded 10%. I felt that STWD’s diversified capital deployment, which included $10.2B invested YTD and a major net lease acquisition, positioned them for earnings growth as rates declined. Management's anticipation of a lower rate environment was certainly bullish, but now things have changed. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am still bullish on STWD despite a macroeconomic landscape that has changed in a way that isn’t as bullish for REITs. The chances of a rate cut this year have almost disappeared, and the spike in oil is causing input prices to increase. Despite these headwinds, I am still bullish and buying, as I believe this is a long-term opportunity to lock in a tremendous amount of yield.

I believe the macroeconomic environment is creating a great setup for STWD

The pullback in STWD has almost nothing to do with the company itself. It’s a macro story revolving around interest rates and oil. Since Liberation Day in April 2025 the market has gone through wave after wave of tariff-related anxiety. Trade policy uncertainty has become the default excuse for selling anything with yield. REITs across the board got hit, and even agency mREITs with zero credit risk sold off. Here is the big thing that many investors and the market are missing. STWD is a lender, and its borrowers are multifamily landlords collecting rent in Dallas and London, industrial operators running warehouses in New York and Dublin, and power plants generating electricity in the Northeast. These are not businesses that get disrupted because the U.S. slaps a 20% duty on European steel. The connection between trade policy and STWD’s cash flow is minimal, yet the stock has traded as though there’s a direct hit to earnings. What tariffs could do is slow economic growth more broadly, and while that’s certainly a concern, STWD’s portfolio is positioned defensively. STWD’s multifamily is now 39% of commercial loan collateral, which is bullish because people still need apartments in a recession.

What drives me crazy about how the market prices mortgage REITs is that investors hear that rates are staying higher for longer and immediately sell anything that looks like it has interest rate exposure. That’s backwards thinking for STWD as 99% of STWD’s $16.6 billion commercial lending portfolio is floating rate. When SOFR stays elevated, STWD collects more spread income. The company earns more, not less, in a higher-rate environment. STWD’s $2.3 billion residential lending portfolio is the rate-sensitivity hedge because when rates do eventually come down, those fixed-rate mortgage assets appreciate in value. In 2025 the residential book saw $122 million in positive fair value adjustments as rates declined. This indicates that STWD is structured to benefit whether rates go up or down, and the market just hasn’t bothered to look that deep into them.

I believe that 2025 was a transition year for STWD and 2026 will be the inflection year

Barry Sternlicht was upfront on the earnings call that 2025 was a transition year for STWD. The Fundamental net lease acquisition in July created temporary dilution, while the fundamental net lease acquisition in July created temporary dilution. STWD’s securitization activity and takeout refinancing led to higher-than-normal cash balances sitting on the sideline. STWD’s full year distributable earnings came in at $1.69 per share compared to the $1.92 that gets paid out through the dividend, which is tight. This included roughly $0.03 per share of temporary dilution from the FIP net lease acquisition, and there were several embedded catalysts that should drive DE higher in 2026. What really stands out is the scale of activity: STWD deployed $12.7 billion in a single year while simultaneously executing $4.4 billion in capital markets transactions. STWD isn’t treading water and trying to stay afloat as they're building earning power for the next several years. The commercial lending book grew to $16.6 billion, and the infrastructure lending book sits at $2.9 billion with an 8.1% unlevered yield. Every dollar STWD’s deploying now is a dollar earning spread income going forward.

STWD held elevated levels of cash through 2025 because of the pace of securitizations and refinancings. That’s capital that was sitting in the bank earning interest from money markets rather than being deployed into 7-8% yielding loans. As management redeploys this capital, their earnings will increase. STWD’s cash drag is temporary, and the deployment pipeline is active. When STWD originates a transitional loan, it doesn’t fund the entire amount on day one, as there are committed but unfunded amounts tied to borrower business plans. This can include renovation milestones, and lease-up thresholds. As these fundings occur, they convert idle capacity into interest-earning assets at existing spread levels, which becomes an organic growth engine that requires no new originations.

The Fundamental net lease acquisition closed in July 2025 and the platform now has 492 properties across 44 states, which are 100% occupied with a 17.3-year weighted average lease term and 2.27% annual rent escalators. In Q4 STWD acquired 16 additional properties at a 7.44% cap rate. After Q4 ended, STWD completed their second ABS transaction at a record 5.06% weighted average fixed rate. This platform is being financed at increasingly attractive levels, and the spread between acquisition cap rates and financing costs is widening. The net lease business was dilutive in the first two quarters post-acquisition, but it’s designed to be a long-duration earnings compounder. I believe that in the next several quarters it will become earnings accretive in a meaningful way.

STWD has $1.04 billion in nonaccrual assets and $624 million in foreclosed properties. These are real estate assets that currently contribute nothing to distributable earnings. STWD has been working out distressed real estate for over 30 years, and once they take control of an asset, they have a roadmap for stabilizing and monetizing it. In Q4 they sold a Woodstar Fund property at their GAAP fair value basis of $56 million for a $24 million distributable earnings gain. Two additional properties were foreclosed before the year ended, and each time that STWD takes an asset from nonaccrual to productive use is an incremental earnings event. STWD is also trading below book value, and there is a $400 million buyback authorization. At the current prices, each share repurchased is immediately accretive to book value per share and distributable earnings per share. The current authorization represents roughly 6% of STWD’s market cap, and it sends the message that management believes STWD is cheap.

STWD’s dividend exceeds 11%, and I have been compounding it into a larger position

Some investors may see STWD’s yield exceeding 11% and run because they have been programmed to think something is broken or the dividend is unsustainable at these levels. STWD has never decreased the dividend and has paid $0.48 per quarter since Q1 2014. Nothing is broken, and STWD has been able to maintain this dividend for more than a decade throughout many difficult environments, including the pandemic and rising interest rates. The yield is elevated not because the dividend is at risk but because the stock price has been compressed by sector-wide fear. When you look at the 10-year average yield of roughly 9.6%, the current 11% tells me that the market is pricing in more risk than normal. I think that risk premium is overdone, and I have been adding to my position to lock in this yield.

I started my position in STWD at the end of 2016 and since then I have received 36 consecutive dividends that have been reinvested. I have been adding to STWD but in other accounts, I have been able to audit my first block of shares to see what the actual metrics are. I started with a purchase of 135 shares, and now I have 311.50 in this account as my share count has grown by 130.74%, or 176.50 shares. My quarterly dividend payments have grown by $80.90 (124.85%) as they have increased from $64.80 to $145.70. I have generated $3,576.18 in dividend income, which is 116.90% of my original investment. My average annualized income was $259.20 when I started this investment in this account. It’s now $598.08 and I am projecting that by the end of 2035 it will be $1,470.06. The dividend continues to grow each quarter by roughly 2.34%, even though STWD isn’t increasing the dividend. I am very bullish on STWD as an income generator, and that’s why I continue to add to my position in other accounts.

The risks of investing in STWD

Just because I am bullish on STWD doesn’t mean that there aren’t risk factors to consider. Within their portfolio, office exposure remains the single biggest risk even though STWD has reduced their commercial book. If the office market deteriorates further, STWD could face additional credit provisions or property impairments despite deleveraging from this space. STWD’s dividend coverage is also in question considering that the annualized dividend of $1.92 wasn’t fully covered by their distributable earnings in 2025. If STWD’s cash deployment slows down and their nonaccrual resolutions take longer than expected, the market may continue to punish shares going forward. Also, if oil stays higher for longer and the Fed increases the chances for a rate hike, I would expect the narrative of a recession to be talked about and the market to sell STWD off more. While there are significant risks, STWD has been here before, and I am excited to add to my position as STWD is now generating a dividend yield that exceeds 11%.

Conclusion

I have been invested in STWD since the end of 2016 and have been covering it for quite some time. I can’t remember a time when the gap between the stock price and the underlying business quality was this wide. The macroeconomic environment is creating a classic dislocation between the stock price and its fundamentals. I don’t believe that STWD is a speculative bet on commercial real estate recovery, as I feel the way to look at STWD is as a diversified, multi-cylinder finance company run by a management team that’s operated through every real estate cycle since 1991. At these levels, investors are getting a dividend yield that exceeds 11%, more than a 10% discount on undepreciated book value, with an authorized buyback of $400 million. This is the kind of entry point that I like, and other income investors may look back on and wish they’d taken advantage of the opportunity.

data.title: Target Hospitality Set To Benefit From String Of Contract Wins
data.description:
Back in February 2025 I wrote that Target Hospitality (TH) was likely inexpensive after its contract losses.

Now that appears to have been the case, as the company has found another way to put their relatively fungible mobile housing assets to use and has announced a string of contract wins. The business focus has broadly shifted from accommodation services for immigration detention with the government to the provision of facilities for those working on projects in extremely rural areas.

These projects can include power projects, mineral extraction, and data centers. In these situations, mobile housing can be useful, as there simply isn't the nearby infrastructure to house workers, and multi-hour commutes make little sense. Note that the company doesn't only provide housing; they also often offer food services and other amenities depending on the contract.

Prior Contract Losses

To recap, Target Hospitality provides modular housing. It's not particularly glamorous, but it's functional. Previously, their contracts were frequently with the U.S. government holding people in the immigration process. However, contract losses lead to a rocky calendar year 2025, as EBITDA fell by 75% year-over-year and utilization for 2025 was 51% compared to 83% the year prior. They still have some government business, but it's now a minority of what they do.

Current Guidance

The company is now increasing its guidance as new contracts are won. This is the latest (as disclosed on April 1, 2026):

Full Year 2026 Financial Outlook:

  • Total revenue between $360 and $370 million
  • Adjusted EBITDA between $70 and $80 million
  • Total capital expenditures between $220 and $240 million, excluding acquisitions

Note that this guidance is likely accurate for 2026, but it's low compared to history, as contracts mature and cycle a 12 months, 2027 should see materially increased EBITDA (we'll get to that below).

Valuation

As of December 2025, the company has a clean balance sheet with virtually no debt. 2026 is something of a transition year with substantial capex as the company sets up for contract wins. There are few vectors for valuing the company.

In 2024 the company received an offer to buy the company at $10.80/share. Of course, that was a very different operating environment, and customers have evolved since then, but it's one yardstick for the business. It was arguably something of a lowball offer when the company was a little distressed.

We can also see that prior to a wave of government contract losses, the company traded closer to $16/share.

Compared to current guidance of EBITDA of $70M-$80M, the company was looking at EBITDA of around $150M in 2025 had contract losses not occurred. This implies EBITDA could ramp further as asset utilization improves. A fair multiple for this business is tricky, as it's something of a commodity player, but perhaps we're in a favorable cycle currently with data center buildouts requiring these assets.

Here are some scenarios:

Normalized EBITDA (based on history) Multiple Per share valuation
$150M 6x $7.93
$150M 10x $13.22
$150M 12x $15.87

Source: author's analysis

Of course, we don't know the detailed terms of the new contracts, and material capex is involved in repurposing assets. Still at $13.88 at the time of writing, the valuation is not obviously cheap today compared to historical values. It assumes a return to EBITDA profitability of the past and a fairly full EV/EBITDA multiple for a business that doesn't necessarily have a great strategic position. Prior to the contract losses in 2025 many believed those contracts were robust and long-term. That proved not to be the case.

Blue Sky Case

I also want to look at what the new contracts could mean in a more positive scenario.

The company has announced $432M of contract wins since February 2025. Plus, the $550M data center hub contract on April 1, 2026. 2026 guidance is for $370M of revenue (high end). It's hard to finesse this exactly, but assuming 5-year contract terms, and not all wins yet contributing to 2026 revenue, maybe the company can do over $520M of revenue when all recently announced contracts are mature in 2027. A 50% EBITDA margin is not impossible on historic trends, suggesting $260M of medium-term EBITDA.

That might imply a $24 to $28 share price, depending on the multiple used (6x-12x EV/EBITDA). Now, of course, various things have to go right for that to happen, and it would be a 2027 scenario at the earliest. Still, that's likely the bull case. That and the market getting somewhat carried away to the extent the stock is now somewhat linked to data centers and AI.

Risks

As in the past, Target Hospitality is subject to contract risk. Contracts can be lost quicker than expected, and their replacement can take time, as we've seen over the past 18 months.

In addition, repurposing assets can be expensive. The company expects material capex in 2026 as it sets up for new contracts. This can impede the flow of cash flow to equity holders. For example, for 2026, $80M of EBITDA less $240M capex suggests $160M cash burn, which is not trivial. Of course, this should lead to some good years beyond 2026, but the company may well need to finance this gap given its current balance sheet. The company appears to have $175M of financing available currently.

The business isn't that well differentiated. Yes, they have regional housing assets that are in demand currently, but it does appear these assets are cyclical, and contracts can be lost as quickly as they are won, as and when customer needs change.

Contract terms are necessarily somewhat opaque. Much depends on management's skill in capturing value via contracting, which investors can never gauge with complete certainty as contracts are typically confidential.

Conclusion

I don't think Target Hospitality is a great business, but it's seeing a cyclical upswing, and 2027 guidance is likely to be beyond guidance for 2026. That should be enough to support the stock.

My main near-term concern is the potential need to finance capex in the near term. I assume the company will be able to receive debt financing on reasonable terms and use their revolver.

This appears to be a cyclical business in the relatively early stages of an upswing that may persist for some time and that the market is not yet fully willing to underwrite the bull case that may play out.

Just as in 2024-2025, the contracts being added could ultimately be lost in the medium term. I'm currently long the stock, but would certainly look to trim in the $20s, assuming the absence of major incremental news flow and the current wave of contracts will not last indefinitely. This is not a stock to fall in love with, but it could be set for further gains from here.

data.title: 5 Relatively Secure And Cheap Dividend Stocks, Yields Up To 8% (April 2026)
data.description:
************

Author's Note: This is our monthly series on Dividend Stocks, usually published in the first week of every month. We scan the universe of roughly 7,500 stocks listed and traded on U.S. exchanges and use our proprietary filtering criteria to select five relatively safe stocks that may be trading cheaper compared to their historical valuations. Some of the sections in the article, like "Selection Process/Methodology," are repeated each month with few changes. This is intentional as well as unavoidable, as this is necessary for the new readers to be able to conceptualize the process. Regular readers of this series could skip such sections to avoid repetitiveness and jump to the section titled "Selection of the Top 50." Readers can also read a more detailed explanation of the goals and the selection process in our blog post here.

************

Introduction and Market Summary:

Currently, for obvious reasons, the market is in a downtrend and has already lost significantly since the start of the Iran war. At the moment, the market is very news-driven and can go up or down in a hurry based on the latest bit of news about the evolving geopolitical situation.

Though it is far from clear how far down the markets can go, as no one can predict how soon the hostilities can end and the global flow of energy and prices can be stabilized. Even then, stock prices have come down substantially compared to just a month ago, and it may be an opportunity to buy solid dividend-paying stocks at better prices.

Obviously, the longer the war drags on, the worse it will be for energy prices and broader inflation. The market is now predicting no interest rate cuts for the rest of 2026. That can not be a positive for many things, such as the housing market and job growth. So, the Feds will be in a tough spot as they would need to balance both inflation and the employment situation. You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

All that being said, does it impact how we should invest? Probably not in the long run. So, buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities or when the prices are low (undervalued) is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

This month, we highlight three groups with five stocks each that have an average dividend yield (as a group) of 4.26%, 6.56%, and 7.92%, respectively. The first list is for conservative and risk-averse investors, while the second is for investors seeking higher yields but still wanting relatively safe dividends. The third group is for yield-hungry investors but comes with an elevated risk, and we urge investors to exercise caution.

The Selection Process:

Goals and Objectives

The main goal of this series of articles is to shortlist and highlight companies that have a solid history of paying and raising dividends. In addition, we demand that these companies support strong fundamentals, carry low debt, and be offered at a relatively cheaper valuation. These DGI stocks are not going to make anyone rich overnight, but if your goal is to attain financial freedom by owning stocks that would grow dividends over time, meaningfully and sustainably, then you are at the right place. These lists are not necessarily recommendations to buy, but a shortlist of probable candidates for further research. Besides, we think each month, if nothing else, this analysis is able to highlight some nuggets that would have otherwise skipped our attention.
