Every month, we start with roughly 7,500 stocks that are listed and traded on U.S. exchanges, including over-the-counter (OTC) networks. Using our filtering criteria, the initial list is quickly narrowed to less than 600 stocks, mostly dividend-paying and dividend-growing. From thereon, by using various data elements, including dividend history, payout ratios, revenue growth, debt ratios, EPS growth, etc., we calculate a "Dividend Quality Score" for each stock that measures the relative safety and sustainability of the dividend. In addition to dividend safety, we also seek cheaper valuations. We also demand that the selected companies have an established business model, solid dividend history, manageable debt, and investment-grade credit rating.

Note: All tables in this article are created by the author unless explicitly specified. The stock data have been sourced from various sources such as Seeking Alpha, Yahoo Finance, GuruFocus, Morningstar, and Investors.com.

Criteria to Shortlist:

  • Market cap > $7 billion (some exceptions can be made)
  • Dividend yield > 1.0% (some exceptions are made to include high-quality but lower-yielding companies)
  • Daily average volume > 100,000
  • Dividend growth past five years >= 0.

By applying the above criteria, we get between 500 and 600 companies.

Narrowing Down the List:

As a first step, we would like to eliminate stocks that have less than five years of dividend history. Also, since we want a lot of flexibility and wider choice at this initial stage, we include some companies that pay dividends lower than the S&P500 but otherwise have a stellar dividend record and growing dividends at a fast pace.

However, so far in this list, we have demanded five or more years of consistent dividend growth. But what if a company had a very stable record of dividend payments but did not increase the dividends from one year to another? At times, some of these companies are foreign-based companies, and due to currency fluctuations, their dividends may appear to have been cut in U.S. dollars, but in reality, that may not be true at all when looked at in the actual currency of reporting. At times, we may provide some exceptions when a company may have cut the dividend in the past, but otherwise, it looks compelling.

We now import the various data elements from many sources and assign weights based on different criteria as listed below:

  • Current yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth)
  • Payout ratio based on EPS
  • Past three-year and 5-year dividend growth
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next five years' growth)
  • Chowder number- This number has been named after the well-known SA author Chowder, who first coined and popularized this factor. This number is derived by adding the current yield and the past five years' dividend growth rate. A Chowder number of "12" or more ("8" for utilities) is considered good.
  • Debt/Capital ratio
  • Debt/Asset ratio
  • S&P's credit rating
  • Distance from 52-week high: This factor is calculated as (current price - 52-week high) / 52-week high.
  • 5-year Sales or Revenue growth.

Selection Of The Top 50

Downloadable Dataset: Below, we provide a link to the table with relevant data on nearly 500 stocks. Readers can download this table for further analysis. Please note that the table is sorted by "Total Weight" or "Quality Score."

File-for-export_-_5_Safe_DGI_-_APR_2026.xlsx

We will first bring down the list (of 400-plus names) to roughly 50 names by automated criteria, as listed below. In the second step, which is mostly manual and subjective, we will bring the list down to about 15.

  • Step 1: First, take the top 20 names from the above table (based on total weight or quality score). At times, some industry segments tend to get overcrowded at the top, so we take the top two (or three) and ignore the rest.
  • Step 2: As a second step, we will take the top 10 names based on the highest dividend yield. When it comes to dividend yield, some of the industry segments tend to be overcrowded. So, we will take the top two (or a maximum of three) names from any single industry segment.
  • Step 3: Now, we will sort our list based on five-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 4: We will now consider more recent dividend growth, as that is more indicative of the company's future growth. We will sort our list based on 3-year dividend growth (highest at the top) and select the top 10 names.
  • Step 5: We also want to give priority to stocks that are rated highest in terms of credit rating. So, we will sort the list based on the numerical weight of the credit rating and select the top 10 stocks with the best credit rating. Again, we are careful not to have too many names from the same sector.
  • Step 6: Lastly, as the name of the series suggests, we want to have some names that may be trading cheaper in comparison to their historical valuation. So, we select the top ten names with the highest discount. However, they could be trading cheap for just the wrong reasons, so we need to be careful that they meet our other quality criteria.

From the above steps, we now have a total of 70 names in our final consideration. However, the following stocks appeared more than once:

Stocks that appeared two times: ADP, CAG, COKE, MSFT, NWG, and VRT (6 duplicates).

Stocks that appeared three times: AU (2 duplicates).

After removing eight duplicates, we are left with 62 (70-8) names.

Since we still have a very large list, we will remove names that have a low yield and not very great dividend growth. So, if the dividend yield was less than <= 2% and the dividend growth (average of 5-year and 3-year) was less than 15%, we would remove them at this stage.

So, we remove COST, PH, MSFT, and CME. After removing, we are left with 58 names.

Since there are still multiple names in each industry segment, we will keep a maximum of two or three names (from the top) from any one segment. The criteria are to keep the names that have reasonable dividend yields, dividend growth, and valuation.

On this basis, we remove a total of 18 names:

FIS, PAYX, TSM, RL, COKE, FMX, BBVA, CIB, TD, BK, IBKR, BRO, ERIE, PGR, LLY, NVO, SM, and TGRP.

After we remove these 18 names, we are left with 40 (58-18) names.

The top names from each sector/industry segment are presented:

Financial Services, Banking, and Insurance:

Banks- Regional:

Banks- Major & Regional: (WFC)

Bank (Foreign): (HSBC), (NWG)

Financial Services - Others: (ARES), (BX), (AGNC), (ARCC)

(ETN), (GE), (VRT), (TS)

(UPS)

Defense/Aerospace:

Cons-discretionary: (TJX), (DRI), (NKE)

Tobacco:

(TU)

Pharma: (MRK), (JNJ), (PFE)

Healthcare Insurance: (CI), (UNH)

Healthcare Services:

Energy:

Energy Majors: (CVX)

Oil & Gas (prod. & exploration): (IMO), (CVE)

Pipelines/ Midstream: (MPLX)

(SBS)

Housing/ Construction:

(GLPI), (ARE), (DOC).

Final Step: Narrowing Down To Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 40, we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments, and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Final A-List (Relatively Safe Income):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 2.75% and 4.25%.
  • At least three or four names with an "A" rating (from S&P)
  • Solid track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 4.26%

  • (ADP)
  • (UNH)
  • (PG)
  • (BX)
  • (PFE)

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 4.26%, which is almost 4x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is 39 years. Also, all five companies have an excellent rating of "A-" or higher. If you must need higher dividends, consider the B-List or the C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details, as that will make the article unduly long.

ADP (Automatic Data Processing)

There has been panic selling of software stocks in 2026 due to the perceived fear of AI takeover of many of the enterprise functions. Investors have sold off their software stocks, irrespective of the quality. ADP may be one such company that is oversold due to general sentiment. Also, the recent market downturn has pushed prices even further down. We agree, there is a valid argument that advancements in AI could result in improved capabilities of corporations to move payroll processing and other HR related functions in-house, thereby impacting ADP's business. However, the fear appears to be overdone and ignores the company's own potential to use AI effectively to increase its business and reach. However, there is a certain level of uncertainty that will remain for some time.

ADP's yield has gone up considerably in recent times due to an ongoing price correction. The current dividend yield of 3.33% is very favorable compared to its 5-year average of 2%. It is often considered a favorite of dividend investors, given the company's stability and the reliability of the dividend and above-average dividend growth. The current correction may be offering a unique opportunity.

UNH (UnitedHealth Group):

UnitedHealth is an integrated healthcare company that operates in multiple business segments, such as healthcare insurance, pharmacy benefit management, health provider, and health analytics. Until last year, this approach provided an outstanding record of performance and shareholder returns. However, the company fell from grace with many allegations surrounding its healthcare denial practices and controversies around its Medicare Advantage programs. This has led to an antitrust investigation along with an intense regulatory scrutiny targeted at the company's practices.

Due to the ongoing issues, the market price fell sharply in 2025 by nearly 55%, and has been range-bound since then. Over the last year, Morningstar has adjusted its fair value estimate for the company. Still, the current price sits over 35% below Morningstar’s estimate.

Sure, the company is facing many headwinds, and margins in some of the segments may be permanently lower, but patient investors can still benefit from a possible turnaround down the road. In the meantime, a 3.3% dividend yield can help. So, only patient investors should consider it after due diligence.

PG (Procter & Gamble Co)

Procter & Gamble is a well-known and wide-moat company in the consumer staples segment. Its brands are well known, but the company’s growth has been tepid in recent times. Also, the margins have been under pressure due to inflationary trends since 2021. However, all that said, the company enjoys a wide moat with its vast and well-entrenched brands. The company’s stock price is fairly valued (so, not undervalued), but it provides a decent level of nearly 3% dividend yield that is stable. As a whole, it should be a good, large-cap dividend-paying stock to hold in volatile times.

BX (Blackstone Inc):

We included Blackstone in our A-List last month as well. The company is one of the most respected alternative-asset managers. It has roughly $1.2 trillion in AUM (assets under management), out of which roughly over 72% generate fee-based revenue. Its major business/product lines include private equity, real estate (or real assets), private credit, and other alternatives. Blackstone has specialized in revitalizing property assets or companies by various means. Even though the fundraising by alternative asset managers was challenging during 2022-2025 due to elevated interest rates, Blackstone was able to raise $150 billion or more in each of the three past years.

The company's shares have lost over 25% since the beginning of this year, mostly due to adverse industry-wise cyclical headwinds. Some of the decline is related to the recent overall market decline. That said, the company has faced such issues before, and due to its scale and management excellence, it has come out stronger each time. So, it may be a good time to open a position. In the meantime, the company generates a lot of fee-based revenue and pays over 80% of that in the form of dividends. The current dividend yield is 5.44%, but the dividend amount can vary greatly from year to year. The dividend growth for this company can also be patchy, but it has still managed to grow 13% annualized in the last 5-6 years (since 2020). Morningstar assigns it a wide-moat rating in its industry and assigns a fair value nearly 25% above its current price, even though Morningstar has lowered its fair value estimate.

PFE (Pfizer):

We are including Pfizer in the A-list and B-list this month. The reason is obviously the attractive and tempting yield of 6.25%. We always tend to include one company in the A-list with a slightly higher yield. However, we try to minimize the risks. The stock price of PFE has been so beaten down that the recent market correction (due to the Iran war) has not had much impact on its share price.

The health and pharma sector was highly undervalued in 2024/2025, due to many ongoing uncertainties, but those uncertainties are gradually fading away. This has been reflected in the recovery of the sector in 2026 (prior to the market downturn). This has benefited Pfizer's stock price to some extent, and it has gained significantly and much above the lows of 2025. However, the prices and the yield are still attractive. In fact, the yield is still the highest amongst the major pharma companies, but the dividend growth is tepid. Further, Morningstar assigns it a 4-star rating, and the current price is nearly 15% below its estimate of fair value. The company enjoys an excellent credit rating, even though the acquisition of Seagen in 2023 and Metsera in 2025 added to Pfizer's debt.

Pfizer's pipeline has been improving with several successful recent drug launches and acquisitions. There will be some patent losses between 2026 and 2028, but the major ones will be towards the end of 2028. So, the company has ample time and potential to replace them with the new drugs.

Final B-List (High Yield, Moderately Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • Yield for the group between 4.75% to 6.75%.
  • At least two names with 'A-' (or above) rated.
  • Above-average track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 6.56%

  • (ADP)
  • (UNH)
  • (PFE)
  • (ARCC)
  • (CAG)

Note 1: Very often, we include a few low-risk stocks in the B-list and C-list. Also, oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. This is done on purpose. We try to make each of our lists fairly diversified among different sectors/industry segments of the economy. We try to include a few of the highly conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1B: B-LIST (High Yield)

=====

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield of 6.6% and an average of 33 years of dividend history and 12 years (average) of consecutive growth.

Some of our high-yield selections often repeat in the B-list and C-list.

ARCC (Ares Capital)

ARCC is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset value. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. Besides, ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high and low-interest-rate environments. The company offers a stable dividend. The current environment is somewhat challenging to say the least, and that's why the valuation has come down significantly and offers a good opportunity for long-term investors.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net Investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1.2 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers a highly attractive yield of roughly 10.7%. The most recent NII for the last quarter was $0.52 a share, which was still above the quarterly dividend of $0.48 a share, though the margin of coverage is much lower.

CAG (Conagra Brands)

Conagra offers a very attractive dividend yield of 9.2%. At first, we should be alarmed by that level of dividend yield from a consumer staples company. So, apparently, there are risks that investors should be aware of before they make investments in this company. The company's stock price has been in a constant downtrend all of this year and has lost more than 50% since 2023. The stock has lost over 33% in 2025 alone while the S&P500 gained 19% in the same time period. However, most of the downside appears to be priced in, and further downside may be limited, if any, unless the broader market keeps declining due to the geopolitical situation.

Now, some reality with negatives and risks. The company does not enjoy any moat and is facing stiff competition and pricing pressures from private labels. Moreover, its balance sheet is not in a very strong situation with net debt/adjusted-EBITDA of nearly 3.8x. Also, the dividend payout is somewhat elevated at 73%; however, the company is at least targeting to bring it down to 55% in the next few years. With that outlook, there is high income with potentially elevated risks. Only income investors with high risk tolerance should consider this stock.

Final C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Less Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 7.0% to 8.0%
  • All names with investment-grade ratings.
  • Some potential for growth.

Average yield: 7.92%

  • (BX)
  • (MPLX)
  • (GLPI)
  • (ARCC)
  • (CAG)

Notes:

Note 1: Oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. We try to include one or two conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1C: C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Elevated Risk)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

=====

It is apparent from the selections that this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up to 7.9%. However, please note that this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even the B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but not nearly as safe as in the A-List or even the B-list. At least a couple of companies in this list may have an elevated level of risk, as they are sometimes turnaround stories. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. Even then, it's a diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

MPLX (MPLX LP)

MPLX LP makes it to our B-list and C-list on a regular basis. It is a highly attractive name from the midstream energy industry. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in case of regular dividends.

We have covered MPLX multiple times in our previous articles in this monthly series. Hence, we will skip the details here. MPLX regularly features on our C-list (and sometimes on the B-list) due to its high income, stable business, and decent dividend growth. Also, there are other good alternatives, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) or Energy Transfer (ET).

GLPI (Gaming and Leisure Properties):

GLPI is a real estate investment property trust, and it specializes in acquiring, financing, owning, and leasing real estate properties related to the gaming and leisure industry. GLPI leases its properties to gaming operators in triple-net lease arrangements.

GLPI came into existence as a REIT in 2013 due to the spinoff from Penn National Gaming. Since then, it has continually expanded its operations at a steady pace and now owns a portfolio of 69 gaming properties with over 15,000 hotel rooms spanning across 20 states. Most GLPI properties are in the northeastern and south-central parts of the United States. Its portfolio is considered high quality, with several tenants ranked number one in their respective markets. The only other publicly traded REIT in the gaming industry is VICI Properties (VICI), which is much larger than GLPI in terms of its assets and market cap.

GLPI's occupancy rates have been extremely good at 100% for most of its existence. It has a total of eight unique tenants; however, over 87% of the rents are received from just four tenants/operators. So, that may look like a huge negative, but it has had zero rent defaults so far. In addition, this is just the nature of the gaming industry, as there are only a limited number of operators.

The company is currently paying a quarterly dividend of $0.78 per share, which increased from $0.76 per share in the year-ago period (and $0.73 two years ago). So, its dividend is $3.12 a year, and the AFFO in 2025 was $3.88, covering the dividend at 1.24x. The AFFO guidance for 2026 is between $4.06 to $4.11.

GLPI is a very stable cash-flow generator and a reliable dividend payer. With the exception of 2020, when it cut the dividends by about 10%, it has raised the dividends almost every year. It also pays a small amount in special dividends sometimes. The average dividend growth has been in the low single digits over the last 5 years and 10 years. The current yield is very attractive at 6.95% and much better than its five-year average of roughly 6%.

Note: We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.

What If We Were to Combine the Three Lists?

If we combine the three lists and remove the duplicates (because of combining), we would be left with nine names.

Two-time duplicates: ADP, ARCC, BX, CAG, PFE, and UNH (6 duplicates).

After removing these SIX duplicates, we are left with nine names.

The combined list is highly diversified in as many as nine different industry segments. However, there may be multiple names for some of the sectors. The stats for the group of 9 are as follows:

Average yield: 6.2%

Average discount (from 52WK High): -26.7%

Average 5-yr dividend growth: 7.9%.

Average Payout Ratio: 72%.

Average Total Quality Score: 65.4.

Table 3:

Conclusion:

In this article, we have presented three groups of dividend-paying stocks (five each) with different goals in mind to suit the varying needs of a wider audience. Even though the risk profile of each group is different, each group in itself is fairly balanced and diversified.

This month, the first group yields 4.26%, while the second group elevates the yield to 6.56%. We also presented a C-list for yield-hungry investors with a 7.92% yield. The combined group (all three lists combined with duplication removed) offers an even more diversified group with nine positions and a 6.16% yield.

data.title: Zoetis: Deep Value Persists
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Are you more of a dog or a cat person?

It's not an uncommon question within the United States, and most people have an immediate answer. According to the American Veterinary Medical Association, Americans favor dogs over cats. The same source reports that over 87 million dogs and 76 million cats are owned by Americans. This equates to 42.6% of households owning a dog and 32.6% owning a cat. Ironically, cat owners usually own more cats per home than dog owners do.

With well over 150 million cats and dogs owned within the United States, demand for their food, toys, and medical care remains a consistent market for companies to address effectively.

As a dividend investor focused on maximizing total returns and buying growing dividends, I am always on the lookout for attractively priced companies in the market that meet every type of persistent consumer need.

Today, I want to look at a company meeting pet owners where they have a consistent need: the medical needs of their pets.

Let's dive in!

Potential Blockbuster Candidates Can Drive Continued Growth

Our investment thesis in Zoetis (ZTS) remains similar to when we last highlighted it in January: the company has hundreds of product lines in seven major product categories, which provides it with broad diversification.

Unsurprisingly, the company is diversified in more than just its product lines: geographically, almost 55% of its $9.47 billion in 2025 revenue was derived in the U.S., with the remaining 45% being generated in international markets (page 3 of 110 of ZTS's 10-K Filing).

In Q4 2025, the company proved its resilience. ZTS's revenue edged 3% higher year-over-year to $2.39 billion in the quarter. This came in about $25 million ahead of Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter. While the U.S. market experienced softness (organic revenue was flat), international markets thrived, posting 7% organic growth for the quarter.

ZTS's key growth driver was again the powerhouse parasiticide franchise, Simparica, with the Trio product topping $1 billion in annual U.S. sales alone (3% operational growth in Q4 2025). Dermatology franchises Apoquel and Cytopoint continued to be major contributors, but growth slowed to just 1% in Q4 2025 ($426 million in Q4 2025 revenue).

ZTS's adjusted diluted EPS rose by 5.7% over the year-ago period to $1.48 in the fourth quarter. This surpassed the analyst consensus by $0.08. The company's non-GAAP net profit margin held above 27% during the quarter. Combined with a 2.8% reduction in the share count in Q4 2025, that's what helped adjusted diluted EPS growth outpace revenue growth for the quarter.

Looking ahead, two commercial launches are the most notable catalysts in 2026. Last December, the next-generation osteoarthritis medicine for cats called Portela was approved in Canada (following approval in the European Union). Unlike Solensia, which required monthly injections, this is designed to provide three months of relief with a single shot. The longer-acting profile is a big compliance win because many cat owners find frequent vet visits stressful.

Last November, the next-generation osteoarthritis medicine for dogs known as Lenivia was approved by the European Commission. This should also yield improvements in compliance because only one injection is required every three months versus Librela, which is a monthly injection.

ZTS's BBB+ S&P credit rating with a stable outlook also provides it with a low cost of capital to seek out bolt-on acquisitions to further complement the business. The latest example was the $160 million acquisition of Neogen's animal genomics business announced earlier this month to enhance its capabilities to deliver predictive insights, individualized care, and more value to customers.

That's why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted diluted EPS to grow by 8.4% annually through 2028, off a 2025 base of $6.41.

ZTS's 1.8% dividend yield is reasonably attractive. This is especially the case when considering that its adjusted diluted EPS payout ratio is likely to be in the low-30% range for 2026. That should set ZTS up to lengthen its 12-year dividend growth streak with at least 8% or 9% annual dividend growth (for a Chowder number of at least 10 or 11).

ZTS is also a clear value. From the current $117 share price (April 2nd, 2026), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 16.5. For more color, this is about half of the FAST Graphs' 10-year average P/E ratio of 31.8.

Of course, we don't believe that the fair value multiple in the years ahead will be even close to 30. In our view, a more attainable fair value P/E ratio is around 23.

This factors in the fact that ZTS's 8.4% forward annual adjusted EPS growth consensus is meaningfully lower than the 10-year CAGR of 13.7%. Even so, the forward 12-month multiple is so low that the stock is priced at over a 25% discount to our fair value per share estimate of $164.

If ZTS returns to fair value and lives up to growth projections, it could generate a 42% total return by the end of March 2027. Through 2031, ZTS could log 14.86% annual total returns.

Key risks to the company center on its Librela and Solensia osteoarthritis pain franchises. As we discussed in January, there were more than 12,000 adverse drug events reported in dogs for Librela and another 6,000-plus incidents reported for cats on Solensia between May 2023 and March 2025.

Now, reported incidents and causal links are two entirely different things. Still, if vets become skeptical about prescribing the medicines or pet owners refuse the prescription, that could weigh on the franchise's sales. Fortunately, ZTS does have a diversified product portfolio to at least partially counter this risk.

Another risk to the company is a cooling market. A whitepaper from Vetsource, analyzing transaction data from thousands of veterinary practices, found that year-over-year visits were down 3.1% in 2025.

This demonstrates that the post-pandemic boom in pet ownership has cooled off, with many pet owners showing higher price sensitivity for routine care due to tighter household budgets. That caps the company's ability to drive growth through price increases.

Conclusion

ZTS is an undervalued and attractive opportunity to invest in the medical care of animals all around the world. There is a persistent and consistent need for treatments to avoid ticks, fleas, heartworms, and other pests that are attracted to domesticated animals. Furthermore, ZTS has a portfolio of other medical offerings that it's able to use to try to aid in the betterment of the lives of pets. Make no mistake, ZTS is not a charity that is seeking to simply help every animal in the world. It's a business driving to make profits, use those profits to reward their shareholders, and conduct further research to find additional profitable avenues of medical care for animals.

ZTS also owns a wide database of animal DNA through its BasePaws DNA tests, enabling it to see where there may be persistent issues in house pets all around the world. This DNA database is of extreme value and enables ZTS to spot trends or patterns in the DNA that is sent to them from paying customers.

At the end of the day, finding dividends and total returns from companies that meet consumers where there is a persistent need and meet those consumers effectively is a massive benefit to your portfolio. Their Simparica Trio product line is extremely popular among pet owners and is a massive profit driver for ZTS. Likewise, it can be a massive reward in your own personal portfolio as well.
